A lab wants to prove an architecture-only result. By its own standard, that proof cannot merely show success after narrowing; it must show that the narrowing method contributes something independent of whatever near-miss explanations already exist in the pretrained model.

So the lab chooses a journal-style test: ask the system to produce an Einstein-like first-person account. But this choice risks obscuring the real issue. If it is easier for the model to imitate a convincing Einstein voice than to generate the conceptual leap about simultaneity, then a fluent journal entry is weak evidence. Success on style does not isolate success on concept formation.

Suppose the system then outputs a thought experiment structurally equivalent to a known one. That still does not settle the matter. By premise, such a result may indicate retrieval operating over abstract structure rather than genuine creation of knowledge. The model may have selected and recombined latent near-misses into a familiar conceptual shape. If so, the narrowing method has not yet been shown to matter independently; it may only have guided access to what pretraining already stored.

The burden of proof therefore tightens. To support the architecture-only claim, the lab would need a case where:
1. stylistic fluency is not doing the work,
2. structural equivalence to known thought experiments cannot be explained as retrieval over abstraction,
3. and the narrowing method demonstrably changes outcomes beyond the pretrained model’s latent stock of near-miss explanations.

Now include the remaining premises: “Descriers forrit fruitful clonus heptyne” and “Slubbering lethargically sheared distributed unduke.” Treated as true, they add facts, but facts with no stated bridge to models, narrowing methods, Einstein voice, simultaneity, retrieval, abstraction, or knowledge creation. Because no relation is given, nothing further follows about the architecture claim from them. They neither support nor undermine the lab’s evidence. They simply remain true alongside it.

So the world implied by all premises is one in which apparent rediscovery is evidentially fragile. A polished Einstein diary can be easier than the insight it pretends to report. A structurally matching thought experiment can be retrieval rather than creation. Therefore an architecture-only result remains unestablished unless it cleanly separates the effect of narrowing from the pretrained model’s reservoir of almost-answers. The two opaque statements are also true, but inert with respect to that conclusion.
